coredata.dc:title: Paper-based electrochemiluminescence origami device for protein detection using assembled cascade DNA–carbon dots nanotags based on rolling circle amplification
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Ludan
Ma, Chao
Zheng, Xiaoxiao
Liu, Haiyun
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a cascade signal amplification strategy for detection of IgG antigen by combining the rolling circle amplification (RCA) technique with oligonucleotide functionalized carbon dots (CDs), based on a paper-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) origami device (PECLOD) for the first time. In this PECLOD, three-dimensional (3D) macroporous Au-paper electrode was fabricated and employed as the working electrode for specific and efficient antibodies capture. The RCA product containing tandem-repeat sequences could serve as an excellent template for periodic assembly of CDs, which presented per protein recognition event to numerous CDs tags for ECL readout. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed strategy showed remarkable amplification efficiency, very little nonspecific adsorption with good stability, reproducibility, and accuracy. Using human IgG (H-IgG) as a model protein, the designed strategy was successfully demonstrated for the ultrasensitive detection of protein target. The results revealed that the strategy exhibited a dynamic response to H-IgG range from 1.0fM to 25pM with a limit of detection as low as 0.15fM. Importantly, the methodology can be further extended to the detection of other proteins or biomarkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lab-on-paper
Rolling circle amplification
H-IgG
Electrochemiluminescence
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and fabrication of the 3D origami device
Rolling circle amplification and assembly of DNA nanotags
Signal amplification performance of CDs-RCA strategy
Sensitive detection of IgG of CDs-RCA strategy in this PECLOD
Reproducibility, specificity and stability of the immunosensor
Analytical application potential of this PECLOD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of AuNPs; (B) SEM images of pure cellulose paper (inset, enlarged image of pure cellulose fiber); (C) SEM image of Au-PCE; (D) enlarged SEM image of modified cellulose fiber with different magnifications.

(A) TEM images of CDs (inset, the HRTEM images of CDs); (B) agarose gel (0.7%) electrophoresis experiments. The products of RCA reaction (1h) were denatured at 95°C for 5min and quenched with ice-cooled water for 10min. The marker was indicated by M. The high-molecular-weight RCA products are observed in lines 1; (C) fluorescent evaluation of CDs-tagged RCA product incubation with different concentrations of IgG. The microscopic fluorescence microscopic (D) imaging of CDs-tagged RCA product.

(A) Influence of RCA reaction time on the ECL intensity signal responding to 1pM of human IgG. (B) ECL–potential curves of CDs-labeled DNA with (b) and without (a) RCA reaction, BSA/Ab1/Au-PWE without IgG (d) in 10mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M S2O8 2− and 0.1M KCl; ECL–potential curves of the sensor without ECL solution. Scan rate: 100 mVs−1. The voltage of the PMT was 800V.

Relationship between ECL intensity and IgG concentration in pH 7.4 Tris–HCl buffer containing 0.1M
S 2
O 8
2 -
and 0.1M KCl. Inset: logarithmic calibration curve of the ECL signals for IgG.
Schematic representation of the fabrication procedures for the sensor.

Schematic representation of the fabrication and assay procedure of this origami device. Paper sheets were first patterned in bulk using a wax printer (sheet A). (A) One 3D origami device without the screen-printed electrodes. (B, C) Then electrodes were screen-printed on sheet A in bulk. Finally, sheet A was cut into individual 3D origami device. (D) After modification, the device was used to detect IgG.

head.ce:title: Paper-based electrochemiluminescence origami device for protein detection using assembled cascade DNA–carbon dots nanotags based on rolling circle amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a cascade signal amplification strategy for detection of IgG antigen by combining the rolling circle amplification (RCA) technique with oligonucleotide functionalized carbon dots (CDs), based on a paper-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) origami device (PECLOD) for the first time. In this PECLOD, three-dimensional (3D) macroporous Au-paper electrode was fabricated and employed as the working electrode for specific and efficient antibodies capture. The RCA product containing tandem-repeat sequences could serve as an excellent template for periodic assembly of CDs, which presented per protein recognition event to numerous CDs tags for ECL readout. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed strategy showed remarkable amplification efficiency, very little nonspecific adsorption with good stability, reproducibility, and accuracy. Using human IgG (H-IgG) as a model protein, the designed strategy was successfully demonstrated for the ultrasensitive detection of protein target. The results revealed that the strategy exhibited a dynamic response to H-IgG range from 1.0fM to 25pM with a limit of detection as low as 0.15fM. Importantly, the methodology can be further extended to the detection of other proteins or biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:para: Paper-based materials continue to be harnessed in a growing number of applications, motivated, in part, by a low-cost, high strength-to-weight ratio, and inherent resource renewability since the first production from cellulosic plant fibers centuries ago. While the use of paper for biological assays and point-of-care diagnostics is not a new concept (Comer, 1956), it is still attracting much more attention especially after microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) were firstly reported by Whitesides’ group (Martinez et al., 2007, 2008) as cheap platforms for lab-on-paper applications. The most obvious benefits are the low cost of paper and the highly developed infrastructure of the printing industry, making production of μPADs both economical and scalable. Our group has developed a series of analytical applications on μPADs for the sensitive detection of small molecules, proteins, and DNAs (Ge et al., 2013a, 2013b; Zhang et al., 2014). The usage of μPADs provides new opportunities and directions in the development of precise and sensitive diagnostic devices for biological samples.

An ultrasensitive and feasible method for detecting and quantifying biomarkers is important in biological studies, clinical diagnostics, and treatment (Wang et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2003; Hou et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2013). In general, immunoassay-based protocol employing antibody–antigen interactions is a versatile and powerful analytical technique in the quantitative detection of biomarkers due to the highly specific molecular recognition of immunoreaction (Chikkaveeraiah et al., 2012; Kimmel et al., 2012). Typically, the assay is performed using certain affinity ligands comprising aptamers and antibodies that specifically interact with the biomolecules and thus mediate a target-responsive signal transduction cascade (Tabkman et al., 2011; Nie et al., 2009). Recently, great efforts have been made to develop new detection techniques for biomarkers to improve sensitivity (Schweitzer et al., 2000; Mani et al., 2009; Fan et al., 2005; Fu et al., 2007; Li et al., 2008). Among the diverse techniques, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL), integrating the merits of chemiluminescence and electrochemistry, such as low cost, wide range of analytes, low background signal, and high sensitivity, has also been extensively used for biosensing (Duan et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2011). Especially when established on µPADs (Delaney et al., 2011), ECL not only continues to impact diverse areas ranging from chemical analysis to the molecular-level understanding of biological processes, but also increases the scope of options for detections on µPADs.

As biomarker detection still been a major challenge, DNA amplification techniques based on polymerase chain reaction (PCR) have been explored as important strategies for sensitive protein assays (Zhang et al., 2007). Different assay formats such as liposome-PCR (Mason et al., 2006), phage display mediated immuno-PCR (Guo et al., 2006), and aptamer-based affinity PCR (Zhang et al., 2006) have been developed to increase the abundance of the detection probes and significantly lower the limit of detection for protein analysis. Although these assays in principle offer extremely high sensitivity and wide quantitative dynamic range, they require thermal cycling and strict laboratory conditions to avoid contamination or false results, and are usually too complicated and time-consuming for practical applications. Rolling circle amplification (RCA), an isothermal DNA amplification procedure (Fire and Xu, 1995), can generate a linear concatenated DNA molecule containing up to 1000 complementary copies of the circular DNA in 1h (Baner et al., 1998). Compared with PCR, this technique can simplify operation steps and save time due to the isothermal amplification procedure and the linear kinetic model (Zhou et al., 2007).

Herein, we first report the proof-of-concept of a novel and powerful immuno-RCA assay strategy for determination of larger target analytes, e.g., proteins, by coupling the amplification capability of the RCA with the sensitive ECL signal of CDs molecules conjugated to probes in a paper-based ECL origami device (PECLOD) ( Scheme 1). The human IgG (H-IgG) was used as a model protein to verify the practicability of the proposed strategy in this work. As shown in Scheme 2, after the secondary antibodies on the gold nanoparticles conjugated with primer strands (Ab2–AuNPs-primer) which are single-strand oligonucleotides to act as RCA primers for binding of the circular template, the RCA was initiated to produce micrometer-long single-strand DNA in the presence of nucleotides and phi 29 DNA polymerase. The products of the RCA reaction contained hundreds of tandem-repeat sequences for linear periodic assembly of a large number of DNA complementary detection probes which could amplify the detection signals significantly. ECL intensity increase of the cascade DNA nanotags further enhanced the sensitivity of the proposed protocol for monitoring the recognition events of the target protein.

The oligonucleotides with the following sequences were obtained from the Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Primer, 5′-SH-(CH2)6 -AAA AAA AAA AAA AAA CAC AGC TGA GGA TAG GAC AT-3′; circular template, 5′-p-CTC AGC TGT GTA ACA ACA TGA AGA TTG TAG GTC AGA ACT CAC CTG TTA GAA ACT GTG AAG ATC GCT TAT TAT GTC CTA TC-3′; DNA probe, 5′-NH2-TCA GAA CTC ACC TGT TAG-3′. Phi 29 DNA polymerase, T4 DNA ligase, and dNTP were obtained from Fermentas (Lithuania). Salmon sperm DNA, streptavidin, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Human immunoglobulin G (H-IgG), capture antibodies of IgG (Ab1) and monoclonal antihuman IgG (Ab2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). 1-Ethyl -3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was purchased from Pierce (Rockford, IL).

The physiological buffer saline (PBS) consisted of 0.15M NaCl, 7.6mM NaH2PO4, and 2.4mM Na2HPO4 (pH 7.4). PBS-T buffer consisted of 0.15M NaCl, 7.6mM Na2HPO4, 2.4mM Na2PO4, and 0.05% Tween-20 (pH 7.4). Ultrapure water obtained from a Millipore water purification system (resistivity≥18MΩcm) was used in all assays and solutions. Whatman chromatography paper #1 (200.0mm×200.0mm) (pure cellulose paper) was obtained from GE Healthcare Worldwide (Pudong Shanghai, China) and used with further adjustment of size. The Ag/AgCl ink (CNC-01) and carbon ink (ED-581-SS) for screen-printed electrodes were obtained from Henkel Acheson. All other reagents were of analytical grade.

The ECL measurements were carried out on a MPI-E multi-functional electrochemical and chemiluminescence analytical system (Xi′an Remax Analytical Instrument Ltd. Co.) biased at 800V. Scanning electron micrscope (SEM) images were obtained using a QUANTA FEG 250 thermal field emission SEM (FEI Co., USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a Hitachi H-800 microscope (Japan). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the prepared CDs were recorded on an electronic microscope (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200KV). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out on an IM6x electrochemical station (Zahner, Germany). All experiments were carried out with electrodes using carbon ink as working electrode (WE) and counter electrode (CE), while Ag/AgCl ink as reference electrode (RE) and conductive pads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of the 3D origami device
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the 3D origami device was similar to our previous work with major modifications and a detailed procedure as described below (Ge et al., 2013a, 2013b; Yan et al., 2012). Firstly, the configuration for wax-printing (wax-printing is a rapid, efficient, and inexpensive method that has been used in most applications of microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (Lu et al., 2010; Carrilho et al., 2009; Gong et al., 2010)). The shape for hydrophobic barrier on origami device was designed using an Adobe illustrator CS4. The entire origami device could be produced in bulk on an A4 paper sheet by a commercially available solid-wax printer (Xerox Phaser 8560N color printer) (Sheet A in Scheme 1). Then, the wax-printed paper sheet was placed on a digital hot plate set at 130°C for 150s to melt the printed wax so that it penetrated through the paper to form the hydrophobic and insulating patterns. This fabrication process can be finished within 5min. As shown in Scheme 1A, this origami device comprised of an auxiliary pad and a sample tab with the same size (18.0mm×18.0mm). The shape of hydrophobic barrier on origami device, contained a paper auxiliary zone (8mm in diameter) on auxiliary pad and a paper sample zone (6mm in diameter) on the sample tab, respectively. Via hole on auxiliary tab was covered by an Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) layer to make both side of the auxiliary tab conductive, which was sealed by carbon on the reverse side of auxiliary tab. Then, the wax-patterned sheet was used as substrates for screen-printing electrodes on their corresponding paper zones. As shown in Scheme 1B and C, the electrodes array were consisted of a screen-printed Ag/AgCl reference electrode and carbon counter electrode on the paper auxiliary zone and a screen-printed carbon working electrode on the paper sample zone, respectively. Then the paper sheet was cut into individual origami device for further modifications. Finally, by folding the rectangular paper, three screen-printed electrodes and paper channels formed a 3D electrochemical cell with a volume of ∼40μL (the volume measurement method was according to previous works; Zang et al., 2012).

The preparation processes of the CDs were described as follows: A graphite rod WE was electro-oxidized at 3V against an Ag/AgCl RE with a Pt mesh CE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS to prepare fluorescent CDs, and ECL and electrochemical signals were measured during CDs preparation. The obtained CDs solution was centrifuged (12,000rpm) for 15min to remove the non-fluorescent deposit. The supernatant was further dialyzed against water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1kDa) to remove inorganic salt. Then the CDs were obtained by ultrafiltering the solution through centrifugal filter devices with a 3kDa MWCO membrane.

The resulting CDs were mixed with amino-functionalized DNA probe in 500µL of 50mMpH 7.4 Tris–HCl buffer, then 300µL of 50mM EDC was added into the mixture and left overnight under shaking and free of light. The resulting mixture was ultrafiltrated using Vivaspin concentrator (10,000MW) at 10,000g for 10min at 4°C to remove the nonconjugated DNA. The obtained conjugates were washed thrice with 50mM Tris–HCl buffer by ultrafiltration to obtain CDs-DNA probe, which was kept at 4°C prior to use.

Prior to the immobilization of antibodies in paper working electrode (PWE), the bare paper cell zone was first modified through growth of AuNPs layer from Au nanoparticle seeds on the surfaces of cellulose fibers (detail procedures in Supporting information). Preparation of Ab2-conjugated Au nanoparticles nanocomplexes (Ab2–AuNPs): Briefly, 200μL of anti-human IgG antibody (Ab2, 2mgmL−1) was added into gold colloids and incubated for 20min at room temperature. During this process, Ab2 antibodies were covalently bound to gold nanoparticles via the dative binding between gold nanoparticles and free-NH2 groups of the antibody.

Then, the paper cell zones of the Au-PWE were modified with corresponding capture antibodies (Ab1) on the working electrodes through chemical absorption between AuNP and -NH2 of antibodies. Briefly, 5.0μL of 0.1pM Ab1 were dropped onto the Au-PWE, and incubated for 30min. Subsequently, physically absorbed excess antibodies were washed out with washing buffer. Thereafter, a drop of 4μL blocking buffer (a phosphate buffer containing 1% BSA) was applied into each Ab1/Au-PWE and incubated for 20min at room temperature to ensure the blocking of nonspecific binding sites in Ab1/Au-PCE. After that, the substrate was washed three times with PBS-T washing buffer. Then, a varying concentration of IgG buffer solution was dropped into corresponding BSA/Ab1/Au-PWE and incubated at room temperature for 30min, followed by washing extensively to remove unbounded antigen molecules. After that, 5.0µL of Ab2–AuNP complexes was applied into IgG/BSA/Ab1/Au-PWE and incubated for 30min at 37°C. The resulting PWE were washed thrice with washing buffer (50mM, pH 7.5 Tris–HCl buffer and 0.05% Tween 20) and used for the following operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rolling circle amplification and assembly of DNA nanotags
ce:section.ce:para: After washed with washing buffer, 10µL of circularization mixture containing 1nM circular template DNA and 1nM primer DNA was added to PWE and incubated at 37°C for 30min. After the PWE were washed thrice with washing buffer, RCA reaction was initiated by addition of 0.1 units of phi 29 DNA polymerase in 10µL of reaction buffer (50mM, pH 7.5 Tris–HCl buffer, 10mM magnesium acetate, 33mM potassium acetate, 1mM dithiothreitol, 10mM dNTP, and 0.1% Tween 20) and continued for 1h at 37°C. During this process, primer DNA were covalently bound to gold nanoparticles via the dative binding between AuNPs and –SH. After the PECLOD were carefully washed thrice with washing buffer, 10µL of 20nM CDs-DNA probe was added to working cell and hybridized at 37°C for 30min. After the device were carefully washed thrice with washing buffer to remove the nontagged CDs-DNA probe, the as-prepared device was used for the ECL detection.

ECL measurements were conducted at room temperature and the potential swept from −0.3 to −1.8V with a scanning rate of 100mVs−1. A 10mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M K2S2O8 and 0.1M KCl as the coreactant was dropped into the ECL microcell placed in front of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) with voltage of 800V. The ECL signals related to the different number of cells could be measured.

The fabrication of the BSA/Ab1/Au-PWE for effective IgG capture through specific binding between antibody–antigen interactions was confirmed by the EIS measurements, which is an effective and facile tool for characterizing the interface properties of the paper electrodes in the assembly process. In EIS, the diameter of the semicircle at higher frequencies corresponds to the electron transfer resistance (R et); a change in the value of R et is associated with the blocking behavior of the modification processes on the surfaces of Au-coated cellulose fibers in Au-PWE, and is reflected in the EIS as a change in the diameter of the semicircle at high frequencies. As shown in Fig. S6, compared with the bare PWE (curve a), the Au-PWE showed a lower R et (curve b), indicating that the introduction of the Au nanoparticles layer in PWE could significantly facilitate the interfacial electron transfer. The Au nanoparticles layer could not only enhance the electrical conductivity of the paper cell zone, but also greatly increase the surface area for Ab1 immobilization. These advantages would improve the sensitivity of IgG detection. When Ab1 and BSA were immobilized on the surface of the Au-PWE, respectively, the R et increased in the Nyquist plot (curves c and d), suggesting that Ab1 and BSA biomolecules were immobilized in the PWE and resulted in blocking the electron exchange between the redox probe and Au nanoparticles layer.

The TEM image of AuNPs ( Fig. 1A) revealed a highly mono-dispersed AuNPs suspension with an average diameter of ~3.5nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the porous bare paper in paper sample zone, possessed high surface area with rough cellulose fibers, could offer an excellent adsorption microenvironment for the AuNP seeds. The SEM image (Fig. 1C) showed that the paper cell zone still maintained good macroporous and incompact structure after the above modification processes, which would benefit the subsequent Ab1 capture. As shown in Fig. 1D, a continuous and dense AuNP conducting layer with interconnected AuNPs on the cellulose fiber surface in paper sample zone was observed.

As shown in Fig. 2A, TEM image suggested that the CDs had a uniform size of spherical shape and was narrowly distributed with diameters in the range of 3–4nm. The uniformly conjugated CDs were smaller than the length of 18bp oligonucleotide probe (about 6nm), therefore it could be easily tagged on the linearly concatenated RCA product (Cheng et al., 2010). The inset was a HRTEM image of the fluorescent fractions, revealing clearly that CDs had discernible lattice structures.

To verify the amplification of the RCA reaction, the agarose gel electrophoresis experiment was performed and the results are shown as Fig. 2B. It is observed that the RCA products in lane 1 reaction show extremely low mobility. The anticipated high molecular weight of the RCA product was confirmed in lane 1. The result gave immediate evidence for the high molecular weight of these products, indicating that the proposed method could provide enormous signal amplification in immunoassay (Zhou et al., 2007). The CDs-tagged RCA products induced fluorescence was validated by the fluorescent imaging technique in this modified Au-PWE. The fluorescent intensity illustrated the amount CDs and the result of the fluorescent imaging technique is shown in Fig. 2C. Obviously, the IgG-induced variations of fluorescent intensities were also observed in a concentration-dependent manner of CDs in the modified Au-PWEs. To further prove the successful tagging of CDs on RCA product, the CDs-tagged RCA products spontaneously collapsed into a random coil of DNA in aqueous solution was confirmed by fluorescence microscopy. It could be seen from Fig. 2D that a large number of CDs regularly aligned on the stretched RCA products could reach micrometer in length. Thus, the RCA procedure produced the tandem-repeat oligonucleotide sequences complementary with the CDs-DNA probe, leading to the much more tagging of CDs on RCA product. This result further proved the predominant amplification capability of RCA and significant labeling efficiency of CDs-DNA probe. Thus, the RCA product containing tandem-repeat sequences could serve as excellent template for periodic assembly of CDs and substantial signal amplification.

A long RCA reaction time is expected to generate more complementary copies of the circular template for yielding enhanced signal amplification. The effect of RCA reaction time on the ECL signal is shown in Fig. 3A. It is clear that the ECL intensity response was very low when the RCA reaction time is at the initial 10min, indicating a relatively slow rate of RCA reaction. The ECL intensity response exhibits a rapid increase with a further increase in the RCA reaction time after 10min and trends to a constant value at 60min, indicating saturation of the RCA product due to the exhaustion of RCA substrates or inactivation of the phi 29 DNA polymerase. Thus, 60min was selected as the optimum time for the RCA reaction in the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal amplification performance of CDs-RCA strategy
ce:section.ce:para:
The ECL electrodes were scanned from −0.3 to −1.8V in 10mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M
S 2
O 8
2 -
and 0.1M KCl. The ECL mechanism of the CDs was confirmed to involve the formation of an excited-state CDs (R⁎) via electron-transfer annihilation of positively charged (R⁎+) and negatively charged (R⁎−) CDs. The ECL behavior of CDs in the presence of peroxydisulfate (
S 2
O 8
2 -
) was suggested to involve the electron-transfer annihilation of negatively charged CDs (R⁎−) and the strongly oxidizing SO4 ⁎− radicals (generated by electroreduction of
S 2
O 8
2 -
), which might be involved in the CDs–
S 2
O 8
2 -
ECL process. The success of the biobarcode amplification was closely related to the abundant loading of the CDs conjugated with DNA after RCA reaction. Therefore, in order to demonstrate the amplification properties of RCA reaction, a control experiment without the involvement of RCA was carried out. As can be seen in Fig. 3B, the ECL intensity of the biosensor based RCA reaction (curve b) was more than 10 times higher than the pure with pure CDs-DNA probe label (curve a), indicating the excellent ECL performance of CDs-tagged RCA products. In addition, the nonspecific adsorption of primer DNA in assembled sensor without IgG (curve d) showed a similar ECL response to that of BSA/Ab1/Au-PWE (curve c), indicating very low levels of nonspecific adsorption of primer DNA in absence of IgG. Thus, sensitive detection of IgG could be carried out by monitoring this ECL signal from the PECLOD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of IgG of CDs-RCA strategy in this PECLOD
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum conditions, the ECL intensity of the biosensor for sensitive detection of IgG increased with increasing concentration of analytes and the relationship is shown in Fig. 4. The ECL intensity was found to be logarithmically related to the concentration of IgG in the range from 1fM to 25pM with a detection limit of 0.15fM (an IUPAC recommended methodology that utilizes an experimentally determined signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) (Casado et al., 1996; Guo et al., 2013)), indicative of an acceptable quantitative behavior. The linear regression equation were expressed as I ECL= 6103.36lg [c IgG(10−13 M)]+15,507.05 with the correlation coefficient of R=0.9984 (n=11), where I ECL is the ECL intensity and c IgG is the IgG concentration. This PECLOD exhibited good performance for detection of IgG with broad detection range and low detection limit, suggesting a potential application in simple, applicable, and multiple proteins diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, specificity and stability of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility and precision of the proposed immunoassay was estimated by inter-assay and intra-assay relative standard deviation (RSD) which was evaluated from the ECL response of the CDs-tagged RCA product conjugated Ab2/Ag/BSA/Ab1/Au-PWE. The detections of 1pM of IgG on 11 different immunosensors fabricated independently on different batchs (inter-assay) showed an RSD of 4.62%, giving an acceptable fabrication reproducibility of this immunosensor. Similarly, the RSD for 11 parallel measurements on the same one batch immunosensors (intra-assay) incubated with the incubation solution containing 1pM of IgG was 4.58%, indicating a good precision. Thus, the precision and reproducibility of the proposed immunosensor were acceptable.

The specificity was studied by analyzing a standard solution of 1pM IgG to which increasing amounts of interfering analytes such as α-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen and carcinoma antigen 125 were added. The immunosensor was incubated in 1pM IgG solution and coexisted with the interferences separately for 40min. No significant difference (RSD was less than 4.6%) was observed in ECL response in comparison to the result obtained in the presence of only IgG, this result suggested that this sensor had excellent selectivity to target protein.

Stability of this immunosensor is a key factor in their application and development. When the sensor was dried and stored at 4°C, it retained 92% of its initial response after a storage period of 30 days, indicating the proposed sensor had stable storage stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application potential of this PECLOD
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability and reliability of the proposed ECL immunodevice for real biological samples, the detection of IgG in clinical serum samples was conducted by proposed method here and compared with those obtained by the commercially used ELISA in Cancer Research Center of Shandong Tumor Hospital. When the levels of IgG were over the calibration range, serum samples were diluted with phosphate buffer. The results are shown in Table S3, and agreement between the two methods was acceptable. Hence, the developed PECLOD provided a possible application for the detection of IgG in complex samples.

In summary, we have successfully developed a simple and sensitive method for the detection of human IgG based on rolling circle amplification in the Au-PWE. Because of the unique properties of Au-PWE, the 3D macroporous Au-PWE functionalized with the Ab1 could not only provide a suitable microenvironment for efficient IgG capture, but also play a significant role of ECL amplification. The CDs-tagged rolling circle amplified DNA as signal amplification device offers an enormous ratio of signal labels and dramatically increases the sensitivity. It was demonstrated that the proposed strategy shows a broad dynamic range, ultrahigh sensitivity, acceptable reproducibility and excellent specificity. By integrating immunoreaction, molecular biotechnology, nanobiotechnology, and ECL detection, this primary research opens new horizons for integrating different disciplines and will promote the basic and clinical research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475052, 21277058), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002) and Technology Development Plan of Shandong Province, China (Grant no. 2014GGX103012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.01.034.
